questions.question: Why isn't a frontier training run just a sequence of notebooks?
options: Llama 3 405B took 30M H100-hours over ~54 days — one corrupted checkpoint, data contamination, or eval regression can cost a week of wall-clock and a month of GPU budget; survival requires every stage to have deterministic inputs/outputs, a manifest, a hash, and a gate
questions.explanation: Each prior lesson works as a notebook with its own conventions and seeds. The capstone is the orchestration discipline that turns them into one reproducible run.

questions.question: What are the four components of the pipeline scaffold?
options: Manifest (describes the run), orchestrator (coordinates stages and tracks experiments), eval gate (thresholds that decide shipping), and artifact store (hashed stage outputs) — the same shape from 100M to 1T parameters
questions.explanation: The difference between your hobby GPT and Llama 3 is the size of the numbers in each stage's config, not the shape of the pipeline.

questions.question: What is the artifact contract between stages?
options: A legal document signed by the team

Each stage declares what it consumes, what it produces, and how the NEXT stage verifies its input — tokenizer feeds sharded data, base checkpoint feeds SFT, SFT feeds RLHF/DPO branches that merge into the refined policy, which feeds eval and quantization toward the ship gate
A single zip file per run
questions.explanation: The twelve-stage dependency graph makes failure containment possible: a bad artifact is caught at the next stage's verification, not three stages later.

questions.question: Why do eval thresholds act as a SHIP GATE rather than a report?
options: Reports are too long to read
A gate makes the decision mechanical and auditable: if the eval report or served-model checks miss thresholds, the run does not ship — no human optimism overriding a regression at 2am
questions.explanation: Both the eval report and the inference server feed the gate in the dependency graph — quality AND serving must pass before the artifact is releasable.

questions.question: What drives the rollback plan's design?
options: Cost asymmetry: tokenizers and eval reports are cheap to re-run, pre-training checkpoints are catastrophically expensive — the plan maps which artifacts can be regenerated versus which must be protected with redundancy and hashes
questions.explanation: Knowing what a corrupted checkpoint costs (weeks, millions) is what justifies the manifest-and-hash discipline everywhere else.
